---
title: "Practicals: lecture 2"
subtitle: "Behaviours, exposures, PIF and CRA"
author: "Ali Abbas"
engine: knitr
editor: visual
toc: true
lightbox: true
format: 
  html: 
    theme: cosmos
    embed-resources: true
    toc: true
    code-tools: true
    code-fold: false
    page-layout: full
    df-print: paged
    number-sections: true
---

# Learning objectives:

-   Starting from behaviours, calculate exposures for baseline and scenarios
-   Calculate potential impact fraction and
-   Carry out health impact assessment using Comparative Risk Assessment(CRA)

```{r setup}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| eval: true
#| message: false
library(tidyverse)
library(here)
library(gt)
library(plotly)
library(ithimr)
library(DT)
# Read io
io <- readRDS(here("data/lecture_3/ithim_io/io.rds"))
# Read trips from the io object and remove ghost trips (with participant_id = 0)
trips <- io$bogota$trip_scen_sets |> filter(participant_id > 0)
# Set palette for gt table
global_palette <- "viridis" #c("#fee8c8", "#e34a33")


theme_set(theme_minimal(base_family = "Spline Sans"))
theme_update(
  panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
  panel.grid.major = element_blank(),
  axis.line.x = element_line(color = "grey80", linewidth = .4),
  axis.ticks.x = element_line(color = "grey80", linewidth = .4),
  plot.margin = margin(10, 15, 10, 15)
)


scen_colours <- c("Baseline" = '#b15928',
                  "Cycling" = '#abdda4',
                  "Car" = '#d7191c',
                  "Bus" = '#2b83ba',
                  "Motorcycle" = '#fdae61')


mode_colours <- c("pedestrian" = '#99d8c9',
                  "cycle" = '#abdda4',
                  "car" = '#d7191c',
                  "bus" = '#2b83ba',
                  "motorcycle" = '#fdae61',
                  "other" = '#fee8c8')


```

# Exposure Analysis: PA and AP

This section provides a comprehensive examination of physical activity (PA) and air pollution (AP) exposures across age and sex groups.

## PA distribution by Scenario, Age Category and Sex

The following analysis presents the distribution of PA volume, as output from the model, stratified by scenario, age category and sex group.

```{r}

# Start off with mmets outputted stored in the `io` object
mmets_long <- io$bogota$outcomes$mmets |> 
  ## Convert it into long format
  pivot_longer(
    cols = ends_with("mmet"),
    names_to = "scenario",
    values_to = "mmet_value"
  ) |> 
  # Rename columns 
  mutate(scenario = case_when(
    scenario  == "sc_cycle_mmet" ~ "Cycling",
    scenario  == "sc_car_mmet" ~ "Car",
    scenario  == "sc_bus_mmet" ~ "Bus", 
    scenario == "base_mmet" ~ "Baseline"
  )
  )

# Plot distribution by age_cat and sex distribution under each scenario
plotly::ggplotly(ggplot(mmets_long, aes(x = age_cat, y = mmet_value, fill = scenario)) +
                   geom_boxplot(outlier.colour = "black", outlier.shape = 16, outlier.size = 1) +
                   facet_grid(sex ~ scenario, scales = "free_y") +
                   labs(
                     title = "PA Volume Distribution by Scenario, Age Category and Sex",
                     x = "Age Category",
                     y = "mMET hours per week"
                   ) +
                   scale_fill_manual(values = scen_colours) +
                   theme_minimal() + coord_flip())

```

## AP Distribution by Scenario, Age Category and Sex

Analogous to the PA analysis, the following presents AP exposure (PM 2.5 in µg/m³), as output from the model, stratified by scenario, age category and sex group.

```{r}


# Similar to PA, read in PM 2.5 outcome from the `io` object
pm_2.5_long <- io$bogota$outcomes$pm_conc_pp |> 
  pivot_longer(
    cols = starts_with("pm_conc"),
    names_to = "scenario",
    values_to = "pm_2.5_value"
  ) |> 
  mutate(scenario = case_when(
    scenario  == "pm_conc_sc_cycle" ~ "Cycling",
    scenario  == "pm_conc_sc_car" ~ "Car",
    scenario  == "pm_conc_sc_bus" ~ "Bus", 
    scenario == "pm_conc_base" ~ "Baseline"
  )
  )


plotly::ggplotly(ggplot(pm_2.5_long, aes(x = age_cat, y = pm_2.5_value, fill = scenario)) +
                   geom_boxplot(outlier.colour = "black", outlier.shape = NA) +
                   scale_fill_manual(values = scen_colours) +
                   facet_grid(sex ~ scenario, scales = "free_y") +
                   labs(
                     title = "PM 2.5 Distribution by Scenario, Age Category and Sex",
                     x = "Age Category",
                     y = "µg/m³"
                   ) +
                   theme_minimal() + coord_flip())


```

### Individual Travel Behaviour Example

The following analysis examines a randomly selected participant whose PA volume has decreased under the `cycling` scenario. This enables identification of trips that were switched to `cycle` mode and an assessment of their impact on the individual's PA volume. Approximately 10 minutes should be devoted to examining the trip modifications.

The `seed` parameter may be modified to select an alternative participant.

```{r}
set.seed(2)
sample_participant_id <- io$bogota$outcomes$mmets |> 
  filter(base_mmet < sc_cycle_mmet) |> 
  dplyr::select(participant_id) |> 
  sample_n(1) |> 
  as.numeric()

trip_sample_participant <- io$bogota$trip_scen_sets |> 
  filter(participant_id == sample_participant_id)

trip_sample_participant

```

## Exposure-Response and Dose-Response Calculations

This section presents the calculation of exposure-response and dose-response relationships against behaviours for each individual, encompassing both AP and PA.

### PA Dose-Response

The `all-cause-mortality` dose-response relationship is applied in this analysis.

[![All cause mortality dose-response curve](images/all-cause-dose-response.png){fig-align="center"}](https://shiny.mrc-epid.cam.ac.uk/meta-analyses-physical-activity/)

```{r}

RR_pa <- io$bogota$outcomes$mmets |> 
  tidyr::pivot_longer(cols = contains("mmet")) |> 
  mutate(rr_pa = drpa::dose_response(cause = "all-cause-mortality", 
                                     outcome_type = "fatal", 
                                     dose = value, 
                                     censor_method = "WHO-DRL")$rr) |> 
  rename(scenario = name) |> mutate(scenario = case_when(
     scenario  == "sc_cycle_mmet" ~ "Cycling",
     scenario  == "sc_car_mmet" ~ "Car",
     scenario  == "sc_bus_mmet" ~ "Bus", 
     scenario == "base_mmet" ~ "Baseline"
      )) 

RR_pa

```

### AP Exposure-Response

Analogous to the PA analysis, the `all-cause-mortality` exposure-response function is applied to AP.

```{r}

all_cause_ap <- read_csv(here("../ITHIM-R/inst/extdata/global/dose_response/drap/extdata/all_cause_ap.csv"))


ggplot(all_cause_ap) +
    aes(x = dose, y = RR) +
    geom_point(colour = "#112446") +
    labs(
        x = "PM 2.5 in µg/m³ ",
        y = "Relative Risk",
        title = "All cause mortality exposure response function for PM 2.5"
    ) +
    theme_minimal()


```

Using the `ERF` presented above, the relative risk against `pm 2.5` exposure for `all-cause-mortality` is calculated.

```{r}


RR_ap <- io$bogota$outcomes$pm_conc_pp |> tidyr::pivot_longer(cols = contains("pm_conc")) |> mutate(rr_ap =  ithimr::AP_dose_response(cause = "all_cause_ap", dose = value, quantile = 0.5)$rr) |> rename(scenario = name) |> mutate(scenario = case_when(
          scenario  == "pm_conc_sc_cycle" ~ "Cycling",
          scenario  == "pm_conc_sc_car" ~ "Car",
          scenario  == "pm_conc_sc_bus" ~ "Bus", 
          scenario == "pm_conc_base" ~ "Baseline"
      )
 )

head(RR_ap)
```

### Combined AP and PA Relative Risk

PA and AP relative risk values are combined within a single dataset.

```{r}

combined_RR <- RR_ap |> dplyr::select(-value) |> left_join(RR_pa |> dplyr::select(-value))

combined_RR

```

### Combined PA and AP Relative Risk

To quantify the joint effect of `PA` and `AP`, their respective relative risks are multiplied together.

```{r}

combined_RR <- combined_RR |> mutate(rr = rr_ap * rr_pa )

combined_RR
```

### Age and Sex Stratified Combined RRs

Following the approach established in prior exercises, the aggregate sum of relative risk is calculated by `age_cat` and `sex`.

```{r}


grouped_RR <- combined_RR |> group_by(sex, age_cat, scenario) |> summarise(rr = sum(rr))

grouped_RR

```

# Potential Impact Fraction (PIF)

Using the combined relative risk stratified by `age_cat` and `sex`, the Potential Impact Fraction is calculated as follows:

$$
pif = \frac{RR_{base} - RR_{scenario}}{RR_{base}}
$$

```{r}

pif <- grouped_RR |> mutate(pif = (rr[scenario == "Baseline"] - rr) / rr[scenario == "Baseline"])

pif

```

## Disease Burden Data

City-specific burden data, typically sourced from the [Global Burden of Disease (GBD)](https://www.healthdata.org/research-analysis/gbd) database, is stored at `io$bogota$disease_burden`.

```{r}

hb <- io$bogota$disease_burden

hb

```

### Filtering for Relevant Causes

The data is filtered to retain only `All causes` as the `cause` variable and `Deaths` as the measure.

```{r}

hb <- hb |> filter(measure == "Deaths", cause == "All causes")

hb

```

# Health Impacts

## Health Impact Estimation Using PIFs and Burden Data

The PIF for each `age_cat` (5-year age category) and `sex` is multiplied by the burden data to calculate the health burden under each scenario.

```{r}

health_impact <- pif |> 
  left_join(hb |> 
              rename(age_cat = age) |> 
              dplyr::select(-c(min_age, max_age, rate, population))) |> 
  mutate(health_impact = pif * burden)

health_impact


```

## Total Averted Deaths by Scenario

The health impact for each scenario is aggregated to calculate the total number of averted deaths. Positive values indicate deaths averted, whereas negative values indicate deaths attributable to the scenario.

```{r}

health_impact |> group_by(scenario) |> summarise(averted_deaths = sum(health_impact))

```
